original_abstract: While existing theories typically attribute intraparty competition to electoral systems and formal internal rules, this article delves deeper into these dynamics by analyzing the strategic expression of policy preferences. I argue that while electoral defeats often lead to increased preference heterogeneity within a party, candidates who deviate significantly from the party’s median position are penalized with fewer intraparty votes. Furthermore, the findings suggest that party members grant candidates more flexibility to balance competing policy demands when the party holds government office. To test these hypotheses, this study introduces a novel database of party congress speeches from Germany and France, employing automated text classification to estimate the relative preferences of individual speakers. The results demonstrate that party congress rhetoric offers critical insights into internal power struggles and actor motivations. Ultimately, the evidence reveals a sophisticated interplay between governing status, economic conditions, and the degree of intraparty disagreement.
humanized_abstract: Whereas existing research explains the occurrence of intraparty competition through the channel of electoral rules and party statute provisions, this article digs deeper into the matter by focusing on the strategic expression of party policies. Our central contentions are as follows: (1) that electoral defeat increases heterogeneity in party preferences and (2) that politicians with policy preferences that are far away from the party’s median vote fewer ballots of fellow party members; (3) that the party membership gives politicians more discretion to reconcile conflicting party policies if the party is in power. We examine these assertions through an original data set we constructed over speeches of the party congresses of the CDU and the CSU on the one hand, and of the French parties on the other. Using a set of algorithms for the computer-assisted classification of text into categories, we assess the relative policy positions of each party representative who made a speech. Based on these measurements, we derive a detailed picture of the relationships between the party agendas as expressed at a Congress, the distribution of party power, and the underlying context variables, such as whether the party or parties concerned are in government and the state of the economy.
